name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ يَطْلُبُهُۥ حَثِيثًا وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ وَٱلنُّجُومَ مُسَخَّرَٰتٍۭ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْخَلْقُ وَٱلْأَمْرُ ۗ تَبَارَكَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٥٤)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and then settled on the Throne. He draws the night’s cover over the day, which pursues it swiftly, and [He created] the sun, the moon, and the stars, [all of them] disposed by His command. Look! All creation and command belong to Him. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (54)
```

```arabic
ٱدْعُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (٥٥)
```

```english
Supplicate your Lord, beseechingly and secretly. Indeed, He does not like the transgressors. (55)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا وَٱدْعُوهُ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا ۚ إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٦)
```

```english
And do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration, and supplicate Him with fear and hope: indeed Allah’s mercy is close to the virtuous. (56)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَقَلَّتْ سَحَابًا ثِقَالًا سُقْنَـٰهُ لِبَلَدٍ مَّيِّتٍ فَأَنزَلْنَا بِهِ ٱلْمَآءَ فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٥٧)
```

```english
It is He who sends forth the winds as harbingers of His mercy. When they bear [rain-] laden clouds, We drive them toward a dead land and send down water on it, and with it We bring forth all kinds of crops. Thus shall We raise the dead; maybe you will take admonition. (57)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْبَلَدُ ٱلطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُۥ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ ۖ وَٱلَّذِى خَبُثَ لَا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِدًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَشْكُرُونَ (٥٨)
```

```english
The good land—its vegetation comes out by the permission of its Lord, and as for that which is bad, it does not come out except sparsely. Thus do We paraphrase the signs variously for a people who give thanks. (58)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them and are linked to them; for the previous verses were clarifying the dire consequence of polytheism (الشرك) — *the association of partners with Allah* — and the denial of His signs, and that this leads man to eternal destruction and everlasting wretchedness. These verses provide the reason for that by stating that the Lord of all is One, to Whom belongs the governance (تدبير) of everything; it is mandatory upon them to call upon Him and be grateful to Him. The verses emphasize the monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — of the Lord of all the worlds from two perspectives:

First: That He, the Exalted, is the One who created the heavens and the earth in their entirety, then governed their affairs through the most perfect system (النظام) running within them and linking them all together; thus, He is the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين).

Second: That He, the Exalted, is the One who prepares for them their provisions (الأرزاق) by bringing forth various types of fruits through which they find sustenance, creating that through the most wondrous and subtle of methods, which is the sending of rain; thus, He is their Lord, and there is no lord besides Him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ** — "Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days." The discussion regarding the meaning of the heaven (السماء) and the six days (الأيام الستة) in which they were created will come in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — up to His saying — **بِأَمْرِهِ** — "by His command." Settlement (الاستواء) means to be balanced upon something and to be established upon it. It is sometimes used in the sense of equality (التساوي); it is said: "Zayd and 'Amr *istawaya*," meaning they are equal. The Exalted said: **لا يَسْتَوُونَ عِنْدَ اللهِ** — "they are not equal in the sight of Allah."

And the Throne (العرش) is that upon which a king sits, and it is sometimes used as a metaphor for the station of sovereignty (مقام السلطنة). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The Throne (العرش) originally refers to something roofed; its plural is *urush*. He said: **وَهِيَ خاوِيَةٌ عَلى عُرُوشِها** — 'and it lay fallen on its trellises.' From this, it is said: 'I trellised (*arashtu*) the vine,' when you make for it something like a roof. He said: The *arsh* is also a type of litter for a woman, resembling the vine trellis in form. And 'I trellised (*arashtu*) the well' means I made a trellis for it. The assembly of a sultan is called a throne (*arsh*) in consideration of its height. He said: The Throne of Allah is that which humans do not know in reality except by name; it is not as the imaginations of the common folk suggest, for if it were so, it would be a carrier for Him — exalted is He above that — not carried by Him, while Allah the Exalted says: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُمْسِكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ أَنْ تَزُولا وَلَئِنْ زالَتا إِنْ أَمْسَكَهُما مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** — 'Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should falter, and were they to falter, there is no one who could sustain them besides Him.' Some said: It is the highest celestial sphere (الفلك الأعلى), and the Pedestal (الكرسي) is the sphere of the stars. They argued using what was narrated from the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: 'The seven heavens and the seven earths in comparison to the Pedestal are but like a ring cast in a vast desert, and the Pedestal in comparison to the Throne is likewise.'" (End of quote).

Custom has been established since ancient times that the great ones among the rulers of people, their governors, and the sources of their affairs distinguish themselves in the assembly with what is specific to them, such as carpets and cushions, until it led to the creation of beds and couches. Thus, what is called a throne (العرش) was adopted for the king, being the greatest, highest, and most exclusive to the king, while the chair or pedestal (الكرسي) is common to him and others. Usage and necessity required that the king be known by the throne just as the throne was known by the king in the first instance. Thus, the throne became a carrier for the meaning of kingship, representing the station of sovereignty (مقام السلطنة) to which all things return and end, and in which the reins of the kingdom are unified for the governance of its affairs and the management of its concerns.

To clarify this, consider a kingdom inhabited by a nation due to natural, economic, or political factors. They became independent in their affairs and distinguished from others, creating a human society (مجتمع). They mixed and blended through actions and their results, then divided the enjoyment of those results, each specializing in something according to their social weight (زنته الاجتماعية).

It was necessary to preserve this unity and connection formed by society through someone who oversees it. Definitive experience has shown man that diverse factors, scattered actions, and wills, when directed toward a single goal and moved along a single path, do not remain in a state of union and harmony unless the reins of diverse affairs are gathered into a single rein and placed in the hand of one who preserves it and sustains its life through good governance (التدبير الحسن), whereby all are given life; otherwise, they would quickly dissipate and scatter.

Therefore, you see that a progressive society categorizes particular actions type by type, then hands the reins of each type to a "seat" (كرسي) among the seats, such as departments and local particular interests. Then it categorizes the reins of the seats, giving each type a seat above that, and so on, until the matter ends at a single rein presented to the Throne (العرش) and guided to the Master of the Throne.

Among the wondrous aspects of this rein, its expansion and vastness despite its unity, is that a single command (الأمر) issued from this station travels through the stations of the subordinate seats, despite their multiplicity and differing ranks. It takes a form in each station that suits it and is known therein, appearing to its holder in a form from which he benefits and takes as a criterion for his action. The source of the command says, "Let the matter proceed," so the financial interests take it as a financial obligation, the political interests as a political obligation, and the military interests as a defense obligation, and so on, whether ascending or descending.

Thus, all the details of actions, wills, and the judgments (الأحكام) implemented therein, which are spread throughout the kingdom and are countless or infinite, continue to unify and gather in the "seats" until they end at the Throne. There, they accumulate upon one another, merge, overlap, and unify until they become a single thing which, in its unity, contains all the details below the Throne. When this single thing travels to what is below it, it continues to multiply and become detailed until it ends at the actions and wills of the individuals of society.

This is in the conventional, conceptual system (النظام الوضعي الاعتباري) that we have, and it is undoubtedly derived from the system of creation (نظام التكوين). One who investigates the cosmic system finds that the matter therein follows this pattern. Particular events (الحوادث الجزئية) end at particular causes and means, and these end at other universal causes until all end with Allah, the Glorified. However, Allah, the Glorified, is with everything and encompasses everything, which is not the case for a king among our kings, due to the reality of His kingship (الملك) and the conceptual nature (الاعتبارية) of the kingship of others.

In the world of existence (عالم الكون), across its various stages, there is a stage to which all the reins of events placed upon the shoulders of causes return. The reins of causes, despite their differing individuals, types, and the hierarchy of their ranks, is what is called the Throne (العرش), as will come. In it are the forms of cosmic matters governed by the governance (تدبير) of Allah, the Glorified, however He wills, and with Him are the keys to the Unseen (الغيب).

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — is a metaphor (كناية) for His dominance over His kingdom and His undertaking the governance of the matter (تدبير الأمر) in a manner that spreads over everything, small or great. From it emanate the details of the cosmic system, whereby every seeker attains his goal and every need is fulfilled. Therefore, He followed the mention of the settlement (الاستواء) in Surah Yunus in a similar verse with His saying: **يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ** — "He directs the ordinance" — where He said: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ** — "then He settled on the Throne, directing the ordinance" (Yunus: 3).

Then He detailed it with His saying: **يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ** — "He draws the night's cover over the day" — and veils it with it; **يَطْلُبُهُ** — "pursuing it" — meaning the night pursues the day to cover and veil it; **حَثِيثاً** — "swiftly" — meaning a swift and rapid pursuit. In this is an indication that darkness is the origin, and the day, which results from the sun's illumination of what faces it, is accidental to the night, which is the conical darkness necessary for less than half of the earth's sphere opposite the side facing the sun, as if the night follows it and attacks it.

And His saying: **وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ وَالنُّجُومَ مُسَخَّراتٍ بِأَمْرِهِ** — "and the sun, the moon, and the stars, [all] disposed by His command" — meaning He created them while they are disposed by His command, running according to what He wills and for what He wills. All were also recited in the nominative case (*raf'*), and accordingly, "the sun" is a subject (مبتدأ), and "the moon" and "the stars" are conjoined to it, and "disposed" is its predicate (خبر), and the *ba* in His saying **بِأَمْرِهِ** — "by His command" — is for causality (السببية).

The entirety of His saying: **يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ** — "He draws the night's cover over the day" — etc., serves as an explanation for His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — according to what the context provides. This is what most Quranic verses mentioning the Throne provide, as they mention with it something of governance (التدبير) or what leads to it in meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ تَبارَكَ اللهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ** — "Look! To Him belong the creation and the command. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds." Creation (الخلق) is the determination (التقدير) by joining one thing to another, although it later became established in the convention of the religion and its people to mean origination (الإيجاد) or innovation (الإبداع) without a prior model. As for the command (الأمر), it is used in the sense of an affair (الشأن), its plural being *umur*; and as a verbal noun, in a sense close to a person's prompting another toward what he wants, as in "I commanded (*amartu*) him to do such-and-such." It is not unlikely that this is the origin of the word's meaning, then the "command" (*amr*) is used as a noun of the verbal noun meaning the result of the command, which is the established order (النظم) in all the actions of the one commanded, spread over the manifestations of his life. Thus, in man, it applies to his affair (شأن) in life, then it is expanded and used in the sense of "affair" (الشأن) in everything. So the "command" (*amr*) of everything is the affair that suits its existence and organizes its various movements, stillness, and diverse actions and wills. It is said: "The servant's command (*amr*) is with his master," meaning he governs his life and livelihood; and "the property's command is with its owner," and "man's command is with his Lord," meaning his governance in the course of his life is in His hand.

The objection that "command" (*amr*) in the sense of "affair" is pluralized as *umur*, while in the sense opposite to "prohibition" it is pluralized as *awamir*, which contradicts the return of one to the other in meaning, is not valid. Such linguistic variations are many, discovered by the critical researcher. The "command" (*amr*) is like a medium between the one who possesses it and the one from whom it is possessed, like the master and the servant, and it is attributed to each of them. It is said: "the servant's command" and "the master's command." The Exalted said: **وَأَمْرُهُ إِلَى اللهِ** — "and his matter will be with Allah" (al-Baqarah: 275), and He said: **أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ** — "Allah’s edict has come" (al-Nahl: 1).

The Glorified has explained His command (أمره) which He possesses over things by His saying: **إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (Ya Sin: 82-83). He clarified that His command which He possesses over everything — whether it be an essence (ذات), an attribute, or an act and effect — is the saying "Be" and the word of origination (كلمة الإيجاد), which is the existence (الوجود) He bestows upon it, whereby it comes into being. So when He says to a thing "Be" and it is, He has bestowed upon it the existence by which it exists. This bestowed existence has a relation to Allah, the Glorified, and in that respect, it is His command (أمره) and the divine word "Be" (كن). It also has a relation to the existing thing, and in that respect, it is its command returning to its Lord, which He expressed in the verse by His saying: **فَيَكُونُ** — "and it is."

The Exalted has mentioned for each of the two relations — or if you will, for the origination (الإيجاد) attributed to Him and the existence (الوجود) attributed to the thing — various attributes and judgments that we will discuss, if Allah wills, in an appropriate place.

In summary: The command (الأمر) is origination (الإيجاد), whether it pertains to the essence of the thing or the system of its attributes and acts. Thus, the command of the essences of things belongs to Allah, and the command of the system of their existence belongs to Allah, because they possess nothing for themselves at all. Creation (الخلق) is origination from determination (تقدير) and composition (تأليف), whether that is by joining one thing to another — like joining the parts of the sperm-drop to one another, and joining the male sperm to the female egg, then joining nutritional parts to them under specific conditions until, for example, a human body is created — or without composed parts, like determining the essence of a simple thing and joining its degree of existence alone with its effects and the relations it has with others. Thus, the primary origins are determined and created (مخلوقة), just as the composites are determined and created. Allah the Exalted said: **وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً** — "and He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure" (al-Furqan: 2), and He said: **الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى** — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (Taha: 50), and He said: **اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "Allah is the creator of all things" (al-Zumar: 62), thus generalizing His creation to everything.

Thus, the meaning of creation (الخلق) includes the determination of the aspects of a thing's existence and their organization, whether they are distinct and separate from one another or not, unlike the command (الأمر).

Therefore, creation accepts gradualness (التدريج), as He said: **خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ** — "created the heavens and the earth in six days" — unlike the command. The Exalted said: **وَما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ** — "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye" (al-Qamar: 50). For this reason also, He attributed creation in His speech to others, such as His saying: **وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِي فَتَنْفُخُ فِيها** — "and when you would create from clay the form of a bird, with My leave, and you would breathe into it" (al-Ma'idah: 110), and He said: **فَتَبارَكَ اللهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخالِقِينَ** — "So blessed is Allah, the best of creators" (al-Mu'minun: 14). As for the command (الأمر) in this sense, He did not attribute it to others but reserved it for Himself, placing it between Himself and what He intends to bring into being, like the spirit (الروح) by which the body lives.

Look at His saying, the Exalted: **وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ وَالنُّجُومَ مُسَخَّراتٍ بِأَمْرِهِ** — "and the sun, the moon, and the stars, [all] disposed by His command" — and His saying: **وَلِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِ** — "and that the ships may sail by His command" (al-Rum: 46), and His saying: **يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ** — "He sends down the angels with the spirit of His command" (al-Nahl: 2), and His saying: **وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ** — "and they act by His command" (al-Anbiya: 27), among other verses. You find that He, the Exalted, makes the appearance of these things through the causality of His command or the accompaniment of His command. We summarize that creation (الخلق) and command (الأمر) ultimately return to one meaning, even if they differ according to conceptual perspective (الاعتبار).

When each of creation and command is mentioned alone, it is correct for it to pertain to everything, each with its specific consideration. When they are joined, creation is more appropriate to pertain to essences (الذوات), since they were originated after the determination of their essences and effects; and command pertains to their effects and the system running within them through the general interaction between them, since the effects are what were determined for the essences, and there is no reason to determine the determined. Understand this.

Therefore, the Exalted said: **أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ** — "Look! To Him belong the creation and the command" — using the conjunction which indicates a form of distinction. It is as if creation refers to the origination pertaining to the essences of things, and command refers to what pertains to their effects, the states occurring within them, and the system running between them, just as He distinguished between the two aspects at the beginning of the verse where He said: **خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ** — "created the heavens and the earth in six days" — and this is the origination of the essences — **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ** — "then He settled on the Throne, directing the ordinance" — which is the origination of the most perfect system between them by enacting command after command and bringing one after another.

What is sometimes said, that the conjunction does not necessitate distinction, and if it did, it would indicate in His saying: **مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِلَّهِ وَمَلائِكَتِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَجِبْرِيلَ** — "Whoever is an enemy of Allah, His angels and His apostles, and Gabriel" (al-Baqarah: 98) that Gabriel is not of the genus of angels, is refuted. The intended meaning is some form of distinction, even if conceptual (اعتباراً), for it would be unseemly to say "Zayd and Zayd came to me" or "I saw 'Amr and 'Amr." Thus, there must be some distinction, even if only in perspective. Gabriel, while being of the genus of angels, is distinguished from others by his known station, power, and status with the Master of the Throne.

And His saying, the Exalted: **تَبارَكَ اللهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ** — "Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" — meaning He is the possessor of blessings (بركات) which He sends down upon those He nurtures among all who are in the worlds; thus, He is their Lord.

## A Discourse on the Meaning of the Throne

People have followed different paths regarding the meaning of the Throne (العرش), and indeed the meaning of His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — and the verses in this vein. Most of the predecessors (السلف) held that these and similar verses are among the ambiguous ones (المتشابهات) whose knowledge must be returned to Allah, the Glorified. These people consider investigating religious realities and looking beyond the outward meanings (ظواهر) of the Book and the Sunnah to be an innovation (بدعة). Reason proves them wrong in this, and the Book and the Sunnah do not support them. The verses of the Book strongly urge reflection (التدبر) upon the signs of Allah and exerting effort to complete the knowledge of Allah and His signs through remembrance, contemplation, observation, and arguing with rational proofs. The various narrations of the Sunnah, which are mutawatir in meaning, agree with this. There is no sense in commanding the means and prohibiting the result. These are the ones who used to forbid investigating the realities of the Book and the Sunnah — even dialectical theology (البحث الكلامي) which is based on accepting religious outward meanings and placing them according to common understanding, then defending them with available famous and accepted premises among the people of religion — and considered it an innovation. So let us leave them to their ways.

As for the classes of researchers, they have differed regarding its meaning into several views:

1 — Taking the word according to its literal meaning: The Throne to them is a created thing in the form of a bed with legs, placed upon the seventh heaven, and Allah — exalted is He above what the unjust say — is settled upon it like the settlement of our kings upon their thrones. Most of these hold that the Throne (العرش) and the Pedestal (الكرسي) are one thing, which is what we described.

These are the anthropomorphists (المشبهة) among the Muslims. The Book, the Sunnah, and reason oppose them in this and sanctify the Lord of all the worlds from resembling any of His creation or being like them in essence (ذات), attribute, or act; exalted and sanctified is He.

2 — That the Throne is the ninth celestial sphere (الفلك التاسع) encompassing the corporeal world, defining the directions, the starless atlas sphere, which marks time by its daily motion. Within it, touching it, is the Pedestal (الكرسي), which is the eighth sphere containing the fixed stars. Within it are the seven universal spheres, which are the spheres of the seven planets: Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, the Sun, Venus, Mercury, and the Moon, in order, each encompassing the other.

These are what the science of astronomy (علم الهيئة) according to the Ptolemaic system assumes to organize the celestial movements apparent to the senses. They applied to them what the Quran mentions of the seven heavens, the Pedestal, and the Throne. Whatever they found of their properties mentioned in astronomy and physics that did not contradict the outward meanings, they accepted; and whatever they found contradicting the outward meanings in the Book, they rejected — such as their saying that the defining sphere has nothing beyond it, neither void nor plenum; and their saying of the eternity of celestial motions, and the impossibility of their tearing and mending; and that each sphere touches the surface of another without any distance between them or inhabitants within them; and that their bodies are simple and uniform with no holes or doors.

The outward meanings of the Quran and Hadith establish that beyond the Throne are veils and pavilions, and that it has legs, and that it has carriers, and that Allah will fold the heaven like the folding of a scroll for books, and that in the heaven are inhabitants among the angels, such that there is no space of a hide's width but there is an angel bowing or prostrating, entering it, descending from it, and ascending to it; and that the heaven has doors, and that Paradise is therein at the Lote Tree of the Boundary (سدرة المنتهى) where the deeds of the servants end — among other things that outwardly contradict what the astronomers and physicists previously assumed. Those among us who say that the heavens, the Pedestal, and the Throne are the nine universal spheres they assumed, push all that aside by contradicting the outward meanings.

This difference in description did not alert them to the fact that what the Quran describes is other than what those people assume to explain celestial movements, until recent deep research in astronomy and physics, supported by sense and experiment, clarified the falsity of the previous assumptions from their root. Thus, these people were forced to annul their application and withdraw from it.

3 — That the Throne has no external referent (مصداق), and that His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — and **الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى** — "The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne" — is a metaphor (كناية) for His dominance over the world of creation. Often, settlement (الاستواء) upon a thing is used to mean dominance over it, as it was said:

*Bishr has settled (istawa) over Iraq*
*Without a sword or blood shed.*

Or that settlement on the Throne means beginning the governance of affairs, just as kings, when they intend to begin managing the affairs of their kingdom, settle on their thrones and sit upon them. Beginning and taking up a matter, and all that indicates change and transformation of states — though impossible in His right due to His being sanctified above change and transformation — yet His affair is called beginning and taking up in view of the origination of things in their essences and identities at that time. Thus, His affair — which is the encompassing with mercy when it pertains to them — is called beginning and taking up governance, similar to other originated acts limited by time attributed to Him, such as our saying: "Allah created so-and-so," "He gave life to so-and-so," "He caused so-and-so to die," "He provided for so-and-so," and the like.

In this view: While it is true that His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — follows the course of a metaphor (كناية) in terms of wording, this does not negate that there is a real entity (حقيقة موجودة) upon which this linguistic metaphor relies. Authority, dominance, kingship, command, sovereignty, leadership, guardianship (الولاية), and mastery, and all that follows this course among us, are conventional, conceptual matters (أمور وضعية اعتبارية) that have nothing in external reality except their effects, as you have heard from us repeatedly in previous discussions on conceptual matters. The religious outward meanings resemble in their expression our expressions of our conceptual affairs and concerns, but Allah, the Glorified, clarifies to us that behind these expressions are real truths (حقائق واقعية) and external aspects that are not imaginary or conceptual.

So the meaning of kingship, sovereignty, encompassment, guardianship, and others in Him, the Glorified, is the meaning we understand from all these words among us, but the referents (المصاديق) are not the same. There, they have real external referents as befits His holy presence; as for the referents of these concepts among us, they are mental, claimed attributes and conventional, conceptual aspects that do not exceed the imagination. We only established them and adopted them to obtain real effects which are their effects according to the claim. A leader is only called a leader so that those we call subordinates follow his wills and determinations, not because the group is without a reality and he is their head in reality. Nor do we call a part of a coordinated system a "member" because it is a hand, foot, liver, or lung in reality, but so that it may undertake the intended matters in this formation and assembly that a member among the members existing in the human body, for example, undertakes.

This is what Allah the Exalted calls play and diversion (لعبا ولهوا) when He says: **وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ** — "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play" (al-Ankabut: 64). Worldly goals of adornment, wealth, children, advancement, leadership, government, and the like are nothing but imaginary titles that have no realization except in imaginations. Engagement with them for other than hereafterly goals is nothing but engagement with imaginary matters and fanciful forms. Competing to attain them is like children competing to advance in the games they engage in; it is nothing but attaining a fanciful state that has no essence or trace in external reality.

Far be it from Allah, the Glorified, to disparage this fleeting, deceptive life and call it play because of the imaginary concerns it contains, and then He, the Exalted and Sanctified, be the first of players!

In short, His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — while being a representation (تمثيل) by which He clarifies that He has a governing encompassment of His kingdom, indicates that there is a real stage which is the station where all the reins of affairs, despite their multiplicity and difference, gather. This is indicated by other verses that mention the Throne alone and attribute it to Him, such as His saying: **وَهُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ** — "and He is the Lord of the Great Throne" (al-Tawbah: 129), and His saying: **الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ** — "Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it" (al-Mu'min: 7), and His saying: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ** — "and the Throne of your Lord will be borne that day by eight [angels] above them" (al-Haqqah: 17), and His saying: **حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ** — "circling around the Throne" (al-Zumar: 75).

The verses — as you see — indicate by their outward meaning that the Throne is one of the objective realities (الحقائق العينية) and an external matter. Therefore, we say that the Throne in His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — has an external referent, and was not placed in the speech merely to complete the metaphor, as we say in many parables struck in the Quran. We do not say, for example, in the verse of Light (آية النور) that there is in existence a divine glass, a divine olive tree, or a divine oil; but we do say that there is in existence a divine Throne, a divine Tablet and Pen, and a written Book. Understand this.

This Throne, from which it is understood in verses like **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — that it is a station in existence where the reins of events and matters gather, just as the reins of a kingdom gather at the king's throne according to the detail provided in the commentary of the verse, its realization of this attribute is indicated by His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ ما مِنْ شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ** — "then He settled on the Throne, directing the ordinance. There is no intercessor, except by His leave" (Yunus: 3). He explained the settlement on the Throne as the governance of the matter from it, and followed it with His saying: **ما مِنْ شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ** — "There is no intercessor, except by His leave." Since the verse is in the station of describing Lordship (الربوبية) and cosmic governance (التدبير التكويني), the intended meaning of intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — is intercession in the matter of creation. This is the causality (السببية) found in cosmic causes which are intermediate means between events and beings and Him, the Glorified — like the fire intermediate between Him and the heat He creates, and the heat intermediate between Him and the expansion or melting of bodies. Thus, He negated causality from everything except after His leave to convey the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) which the beginning of the verse conveys: **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ** — "Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth."

In His saying: **ما مِنْ شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ** — "There is no intercessor, except by His leave" — is a clarification of another truth, which is the return of any deviation in governance to the governance itself by means of leave (الإذن). For the intercessor only mediates between the one interceded for, who is subject to a judgment, and the one with whom intercession is made, to change through intercession the course of a judgment that would proceed were it not for the intercession. The sun, shining by facing [the earth], for example, is an intermediate intercessor between Allah, the Glorified, and the earth for its illumination with light; were it not for that, the requirement of the determination of general causes and their system would be that darkness encompass it. Then the barrier of a roof or any other veil is another intercessor asking Him, the Exalted, that the sun's light not fall upon the earth directly, and so on.

So if the intercession of the intercessor — which is a cause changing what preceded it of judgment — is based on His leave, the Exalted, it means that the general governance in progress is only from Allah, the Glorified. All the means taken to nullify His governance and change the course of His judgment — whether they be cosmic causes or what man takes of measures to flee from the course of divine causes — all of that is part of the divine governance.

Therefore, we see bad things disobeying and not accepting noble forms and high gifts, due to the deficiency of their potential (استعداد) to accept them. This rejection from them is itself an acceptance, and the refusal to accept nurturing is itself another divine nurturing. Man, despite his ignorance, seeks superiority over his Lord and disdains to submit to His greatness, yet that is itself a submission to His judgment; and he plots against Him, yet he is himself plotted against. The Exalted said: **وَما يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ** — "and they do not plot except against themselves, but they are not aware" (al-An'am: 123), and the Exalted said: **وَما يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ** — "and they do not mislead anyone except themselves, but they are not aware" (Al 'Imran: 69), and the Exalted said: **وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا نَصِيرٍ** — "You cannot frustrate [Him] on the earth, and you do not have besides Allah any guardian or helper" (al-Shura: 31).

Thus, His saying: **ما مِنْ شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ** — "There is no intercessor, except by His leave" — indicates that the intercession of intercession or the opposing causes that stand between the divine governance and its requirements are included from another aspect, which is the aspect of leave in the divine governance. Understand this.

The example of opposing and competing causes and factors in existence is but like the two pans of a scale, struggling by rising and falling, heaviness and lightness; yet their difference is itself an agreement between them in aiding the owner of the scale in identifying the weight he wishes to identify.

Close to the verse of Surah Yunus in indicating the encompassment of governance and the negation of any governor besides Him, the Exalted, is His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا شَفِيعٍ أَفَلا تَتَذَكَّرُونَ** — "then He settled on the Throne. You do not have besides Him any guardian or intercessor. Will you not then take admonition?" (al-Sajdah: 4). Close to His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ** — "then He settled on the Throne, directing the ordinance" — in pointing to the Throne being a station where general governances originate and from which cosmic commands issue, is His saying, the Exalted: **ذُو الْعَرْشِ الْمَجِيدُ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ** — "Lord of the Throne, the All-glorious, Doer of whatever He wills" (al-Buruj: 15-16), which is apparent.

To this meaning points His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهمْ وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ** — "And you will see the angels circling around the Throne, celebrating the praise of their Lord, and judgment will be made between them with justice" (al-Zumar: 75). For the angels are the intermediaries who bear His judgment, implement His command, and act by His governance; so they should be circling around His Throne.

Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** — "Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful" (al-Mu'min: 7). In the verse, in addition to mentioning their circling the Throne, is something else: that there are carriers (حملة) who bear the Throne. They are undoubtedly individuals through whom this high station and great creation, which is the center and source of divine governances, is established. This is supported by what is in another verse, which is His saying: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ** — "and the Throne of your Lord will be borne that day by eight [angels] above them" (al-Haqqah: 17).

Since the Throne is the station to which all the reins of divine governances and Lordly judgments running in the world return, as you have heard, it contains the forms of all events in a summary manner, present before Allah and known to Him. To this points His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يَعْلَمُ ما يَلِجُ فِي الْأَمْرِضِ وَما يَخْرُجُ مِنْها وَما يَنْزِلُ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَما يَعْرُجُ فِيها وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ** — "then He settled on the Throne. He knows whatever finds its way into the earth and whatever comes out of it, and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends into it, and He is with you wherever you may be, and Allah sees best what you do" (al-Hadid: 4). His saying: **يَعْلَمُ ما يَلِجُ** — "He knows whatever finds its way" — etc., follows the course of an explanation for the settlement on the Throne. Thus, the Throne is the station of knowledge (مقام العلم) just as it is the station of general governance that encompasses everything, and everything is within it.

Therefore, it is preserved after the return of creation to Him, the Exalted, for the final judgment, as in His saying: **وَتَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ** — "And you will see the angels circling around the Throne." It exists with this witnessed world, as indicated by the verses of the creation of the heavens and the earth; and it existed before this creation, as indicated by His saying: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَكانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْماءِ** — "It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and His Throne was [then] upon the waters" (Hud: 7).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ادْعُوا رَبَّكُمْ تَضَرُّعاً وَخُفْيَةً** — "Supplicate your Lord, humbly and secretly" — to the end of the two verses. Humility (التضرع) is to humble oneself from *darā'ah*, which is weakness and lowliness. And **secrecy** (الخفية) is concealment; it is not unlikely that it is a metaphor for humbling oneself, brought to emphasize humility, for the one who humbles himself almost disappears from smallness and lowliness.

The previous verse: **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ** — "Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created" — mentions His Lordship alone with no partner from the aspect that He is the Creator alone, and to Him belongs the governance of His creation alone. Thus, following it with these two verses is like drawing a conclusion from the clarification, which is the call to supplicate Him and worship Him, and the judgment to adopt a religion that accords with His Lordship, which is Lordship without a partner in creation or in governance.

Therefore, He first called to the religion of servitude (العبودية), saying: **ادْعُوا رَبَّكُمْ تَضَرُّعاً وَخُفْيَةً إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ وَلا تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلاحِها** — "Supplicate your Lord, humbly and secretly. Indeed He does not like the transgressors. And do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration." He commanded that they supplicate Him with humility and lowliness, and that it be in secret without public display which is far from the etiquette of servitude and outside its garb — assuming the "and" in "humbly and secretly" is for conjunction — or that they supplicate Him with humility and entreaty which usually necessitates some form of public display, or in secret, in a low voice, for that is the requirement of servitude. Whoever goes beyond that has transgressed the bounds of servitude, and indeed Allah does not like the transgressors.

It is possible that the intended meaning of humility and secrecy is public and private, and humility was placed in the position of public display because public display in supplication is contrary to the etiquette of servitude unless accompanied by humility.

This is what is between them and Allah. As for what is between them and people, it is that they do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration. The reality of religion regarding what pertains to the rights of people is nothing but that their affair be restored by the removal of injustices from among them and treating them with what aids them in godwariness (التقوى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and brings them closer to the happiness of life in this world and the Hereafter.

Then He repeated the call to Him and renewed the prompting to supplicate Him by combining the two paths through which humanity has always worshipped the Lord or lords: the path of fear and the path of hope. For some people used to take lords out of fear, worshipping them to be safe from their evils; and some used to take lords out of greed, worshipping them to attain their good and blessing. However, worship out of pure fear might lead man to despair and hopelessness, prompting him to abandon worship, as has been frequently observed. And worship out of pure greed might lead to the release of insolence and the loss of the garb of servitude, prompting him to abandon worship, as has also been frequently observed. So the Glorified combined them and called to supplication using both together, saying: **وَادْعُوهُ خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً** — "and supplicate Him with fear and hope" — so that each of the two attributes may rectify what the other might corrupt. In that is an alignment with the general law running in the world, namely the law of attraction and repulsion.

Allah, the Glorified, has called this balance in worship and the avoidance of corrupting the earth after its restoration "excellence" (إحسانا) and gave glad tidings to those who respond to His call that they will then be among the doers of good (المحسنين), so His mercy will be near to them: **إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ اللهِ قَرِيبٌ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "Indeed Allah’s mercy is close to the virtuous."

He did not say *qarībah* (feminine); it was said: because mercy (الرحمة) is a verbal noun in which both forms are equal. It was said: because the intended meaning of mercy is excellence (الإحسان). It was said: because *qarīb* is a form *fa'īl* in the sense of the object (*maf'ūl*), so the masculine and feminine are equal therein, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **لَعَلَّ السَّاعَةَ قَرِيبٌ** — "maybe the Hour is near" (al-Shura: 17).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ بُشْراً بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ** — "It is He who sends the winds as harbingers of His mercy" — to the end of the verse. In the verse is a clarification of His Lordship from the aspect of the Return (العود), just as in His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللهُ** — "Indeed your Lord is Allah" — was a clarification of it from the aspect of the Beginning (البدء).

His saying: **بُشْراً** — "as harbingers" — its origin is *al-bushur* with two *dammahs*, the plural of *bashīr* (bringer of glad tidings), like *nudhur* is the plural of *nadhīr*. The intended meaning of mercy (الرحمة) is rain. His saying: **بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ** — "before His mercy" — means ahead of the rain. In this is an imaginary metaphor (استعارة تخييلية) by likening the rain to an absent person whose family awaits him, so he arrives with a harbinger before him announcing his arrival.

**Al-Iqlāl** (الإقلال) is carrying. **Al-Sahāb** (السحاب) and *al-sahābah* are the clouds, like *tamr* and *tamrah*. The clouds being heavy (ثقالاً) is in consideration of their carrying the weight of water. His saying: **لِبَلَدٍ مَيِّتٍ** — "to a dead territory" — means for the sake of a dead territory or toward a dead territory. The rest is apparent.

The verse argues through the reviving of the earth for the possibility of reviving the dead, because they are of the same kind, and the judgment of likes in what is possible and what is not is one. The living who were overtaken by death are not non-existent from their root, for their souls (أنفسهم) and spirits (أرواحهم) remain preserved even if their bodies change, just as the plants change what is on the surface of the earth while the living spirit remains in their root, secluded from growth and development, then their active life returns to them. Likewise, Allah brings forth the dead. The reviving of the dead in the universal Gathering (الحشر الكلي) on the Day of Resurrection is but like the reviving of the dead earth in its particular resurrection returning every year. There is a sequel to this discourse that will reach you in another place, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ** — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord" — to the end of the verse. **Al-Nakid** (النكد) is the meager. The verse, in itself, is like a general parable struck for the resulting of righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) and good effects from good and noble essences, and their opposite from their opposite, as preceded in His saying: **كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ** — "As He originated you, so you will return." But in conjunction with the previous verse, it conveys that although people differ in accepting mercy, the difference is from their side, while the divine mercy is general and absolute.

## Narrational Discussion

There has not been transmitted from the generation of the Companions (الصحابة) any real investigation into things like the Throne, the Pedestal, and other Quranic realities, or even the principles of knowledge such as the issues of monotheism and what follows them. Rather, they did not go beyond the religious outward meanings and stopped at them. Upon this followed the Successors (التابعون) and the early commentators, until it was narrated from Sufyan ibn 'Uyaynah that he said: "Whatever Allah described of Himself in His Book — its interpretation (تفسير) is its recitation and silence regarding it." And from Imam Malik, that a man said to him: "O Abu 'Abdillah — **اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — 'settled on the Throne,' how did He settle?" The narrator said: "I never saw Malik as upset by anything as he was by his statement — and he was overcome by *al-ruhadā'*, meaning sweat, and the people lowered their heads. He said: Then Malik was relieved and said: The 'how' (الكيف) is inconceivable; the settlement (الاستواء) from Him is not unknown; faith in it is obligatory; and asking about it is an innovation (بدعة). And I fear that you are astray." And he ordered him to be taken out.

It is as if his saying: "The 'how' is inconceivable" etc., is taken from what was narrated (1) from Umm Salamah, Mother of the Believers (رضي الله عنها), regarding His saying, the Exalted: **الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى** — "The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne." She said: "The 'how' is inconceivable, the settlement is not unknown, the affirmation of it is faith, and the denial of it is disbelief."

This was the manner of their conduct in that; nothing was inherited from them except what is found in the speech of Imam 'Ali ibn Abi Talib and the Imams from his progeny after him (عليه السلام), and we will cite some of what we have found in their speech.

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of narration from Salman al-Farsi regarding what 'Ali (عليه السلام) answered the Catholicos: 'Ali (عليه السلام) said: "**إن الملائكة تحمل العرش ، وليس العرش كما تظن كهيئة السرير ـ ولكنه شيء محدود مخلوق مدبر وربك مالكه ـ لا أنه عليه ككون الشيء على الشيء**" — "Indeed the angels bear the Throne, and the Throne is not as you think in the form of a bed — but it is a limited, created, governed thing and your Lord is its owner — not that He is upon it as a thing is upon a thing." (The narration).

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Barqi, reaching back to him, he said: The Catholicos asked 'Ali (عليه السلام), saying: "Tell me about Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, does He bear the Throne or does the Throne bear Him?" He (عليه السلام) replied: "**الله عز وجل حامل العرش ـ والسماوات والأرض وما فيهما وما بينهما ، وذلك قول الله عز وجل : إِنَّ اللهَ يُمْسِكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ أَنْ تَزُولا ـ وَلَئِنْ زالَتا إِنْ أَمْسَكَهُما مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ ـ إِنَّهُ كانَ حَلِيماً غَفُوراً**" — "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is the bearer of the Throne — and the heavens and the earth and what is in them and what is between them, and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should falter, and were they to falter, there is no one who could sustain them besides Him. Indeed He is all-forbearing, all-forgiving.'"

He said: "Then tell me about His saying: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ** — 'and the Throne of your Lord will be borne that day by eight [angels] above them.' How is that — while you said that He bears the Throne and the heavens and the earth?" The Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) replied: "**إن العرش خلقه الله تبارك وتعالى من أنوار أربعة : نور أحمر منه احمرت الحمرة ، ونور أخضر منه اخضرت الخضرة ، ونور أصفر منه اصفرت الصفرة ـ ونور أبيض منه ابيض البياض. وهو العلم الذي حمله الله الحملة ، وذلك نور من نور عظمته ـ فبعظمته ونوره أبصر قلوب المؤمنين ، وبعظمته ونوره عاداه الجاهلون ، وبعظمته ونوره ابتغى من في السماوات والأرض ـ من جميع خلائقه إليه الوسيلة ـ بالأعمال المختلفة والأديان المتشتتة ـ فكل شيء محمول يحمله الله بنوره وعظمته وقدرته ـ لا يستطيع لنفسه ضرا ولا نفعا ـ ولا موتا ولا حياة ولا نشورا فكل شيء محمول ، والله تبارك وتعالى الممسك لهما أن تزولا ، والمحيط بهما من شيء ، وهو حياة كل شيء ونور كل شيء ـ سبحانه وتعالى عما يقولون علوا كبيرا**" — "Indeed the Throne was created by Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, from four lights: a red light from which redness became red, a green light from which greenness became green, a yellow light from which yellowness became yellow, and a white light from which whiteness became white. It is the knowledge (العلم) which Allah caused the carriers to bear, and that is a light from the light of His greatness — so by His greatness and His light, the hearts of the believers see, and by His greatness and His light, the ignorant became His enemies, and by His greatness and His light, those in the heavens and the earth — from all His creatures — sought the means to Him — through diverse deeds and scattered religions — so everything is borne, Allah bears it by His light, His greatness, and His power — it cannot possess for itself harm or benefit — nor death, nor life, nor resurrection. So everything is borne, and Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, is the sustainer of them lest they falter, and the Encompassant of them in everything, and He is the life of everything and the light of everything — immaculate is He and exalted far above what they say."

He said to him: "Then tell me about Allah, where is He?" The Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) replied: "**هو هاهنا وهاهنا وفوق وتحت ومحيط بنا ومعنا ، وهو قوله : ما يَكُونُ مِنْ نَجْوى ثَلاثَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ رابِعُهُمْ ـ وَلا خَمْسَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ سادِسُهُمْ وَلا أَدْنى مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْثَرَ ـ إِلَّا هُوَ مَعَهُمْ أَيْنَ ما كانُوا فالكرسي محيط بالسماوات والأرض ـ وما بينهما وما تحت الثرى ، وإن تجهر بالقول فإنه يعلم السر وأخفى ، وذلك قوله : وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ ـ وَلا يَؤُدُهُ حِفْظُهما وَهُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ. فالذين يحملون العرش هم العلماء الذين حملهم الله علمه ، وليس يخرج من هذه الأربعة شيء خلقه الله في ملكوته ، وهو الملكوت الذي أراه الله أصفياءه وأراه خليله فقال : وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ـ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ وكيف يحمل حملة العرش الله وبحياته حييت قلوبهم ، وبنوره اهتدوا إلى معرفته**" — "He is here and here, and above and below, and encompassing us and with us, and that is His saying: 'There is no secret talk between three but He is their fourth, nor five but He is their sixth, nor less than that nor more but He is with them wherever they may be.' So the Pedestal (الكرسي) encompasses the heavens and the earth — and what is between them and what is beneath the soil, and if you speak aloud, He knows the secret and what is more hidden, and that is His saying: 'His Pedestal embraces the heavens and the earth and He is not wearied by their preservation, and He is the All-exalted, the All-supreme.' So those who bear the Throne are the scholars (العلماء) whom Allah caused to bear His knowledge, and nothing that Allah created in His dominion (ملكوته) — *the spiritual realm of divine sovereignty* — is outside of these four. It is the dominion that Allah showed to His chosen ones and showed to His Friend, saying: 'Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude.' And how could the carriers of the Throne bear Allah, when by His life their hearts lived, and by His light they were guided to His knowledge?" (The narration).

**I say:** His saying: "Tell me about Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, does He bear the Throne or does the Throne bear Him?" etc., is apparent in that the Catholicos took bearing (الحمل) in the sense of a body bearing a body. His saying (عليه السلام): "Allah is the bearer of the Throne and the heavens and the earth" etc., took bearing in its analytical sense and explained it as bearing the existence of the thing, which is the establishment of the existence of things through Him, the Exalted, in a purely dependent, non-independent manner. It is known that the requirement of this meaning is that things are borne by Him, the Exalted, not bearing Him.

Therefore, when the Catholicos heard that, he asked him (عليه السلام) about His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَةٌ** — "and the Throne of your Lord will be borne that day by eight [angels] above them." For bearing the existence of a thing in the aforementioned sense is exclusive to Him, the Exalted, and no one else shares it with Him, yet the verse attributes it to others! So he (عليه السلام) explained bearing a second time as bearing knowledge (العلم) and explained the Throne as knowledge.

However, since that might suggest a contradiction between the two explanations, he (عليه السلام) added to the clarification of his statement that the Throne is knowledge, that this knowledge is other than what common understandings immediately grasp of knowledge — which is acquired knowledge (العلم الحصولي) that is a mental form — but rather it is the light of His greatness and power that was present for these carriers by Allah's leave and witnessed by them, so that was called bearing. It is, nonetheless, borne by Him, the Exalted, and there is no contradiction, just as the existence of our actions is present with us and borne by us, yet they are also present with Allah, the Glorified, and borne by Him, and He is the Owner who made us their owners.

So the light of divine greatness and His power by which all things appeared is the Throne that encompasses what is below it, and it is His kingship (الملك) over everything below the Throne. He, the Exalted, is the bearer of this light, then those to whom Allah revealed this light bear it by Allah's leave, and Allah, the Glorified, is the bearer of the carrier and the carried altogether.

Thus, the Throne in His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ** — "then He settled on the Throne" — or if you will, the settlement on the Throne, is kingship (الملك); and in His saying: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ** — "and [they] bear the Throne of your Lord" — the verse, it is knowledge (العلم). Both are one, and it is the station by which all things appear and in which the summary of all detailed governances running in the system of existence is centered. It is the station of kingship from which governances issue, and the station of knowledge by which things appear.

And his saying (peace be upon him): "So by His greatness and His light, He gave sight to the hearts of the believers..." etc., he means that this station (المقام) is the station from which originates the governance (تدبير) of the system of felicity (نظام السعادة) in which the community of believers is situated, and upon which their caravan travels in its journey toward Allah, the Glorified. From it also originates the system of wretchedness (نظام الشقاء) that spreads over all the recalcitrant—the enemies of Allah who are ignorant of the station of their Lord. Rather, it is the station from which originates the general universal system (النظام العالمي العام) under which every possessor of existence lives, and by which all of them proceed to seek proximity to Him through their deeds and ways, whether they are aware of the fact that they are seeking a means (ابتغاء الوسيلة) to Him, the Exalted, or are ignorant of it.

And his saying (peace be upon him): "And He is the life of everything and the light of everything," is like an explanatory justification (التعليل) for his preceding statement: "So everything is a carried object (محمول) which Allah carries..." to the end of what he said. Its summary is that He, the Exalted, is the One by whom everything exists, and He is the One who perceives everything; thus, through Him, the specific path of each thing in its journey of existence becomes manifest, just as a dark path becomes manifest to a traveler by means of light. Therefore, things possess nothing for themselves; rather, Allah, the Glorified, is their Owner (المالك) and the Sustainer (الحامل) of their existence.

And his saying (peace be upon him): "He is here and here, and above and below..." etc., he means that since Allah, the Glorified, is the Sustainer (المقوم) of the existence of everything, its Preserver (الحافظ), and its Carrier (الحامل), no place among places is devoid of Him, nor is He restricted to one place over another. The meaning of His being in a place or with a spatial thing is that He, the Exalted, is preserving it, carrying its existence, and encompassing it (محيط به); while it, as well as others, is preserved by His preservation, carried by Him, and encompassed by Him.

This ultimately refers to His active knowledge (علمه الفعلي) of things, by which we mean that everything is present (حاضر) before Him, the Exalted, and not veiled from Him. For this reason, he (peace be upon him) said first: "So the Pedestal (الكرسي) encompasses the heavens and the earth and what is between them and what is beneath the soil," thereby pointing to the encompassment (الإحاطة). Then he followed it with his saying: "And if you speak aloud, He indeed knows the secret and what is yet more hidden," pointing to knowledge (العلم). This leads to the conclusion that the Pedestal—meaning the Throne (العرش)—is the station of encompassment, governance, and preservation, and that it is the very station of knowledge and presence (الحضور). Then he applied this to the saying of the Exalted: **وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ** — "His Pedestal embraces the heavens and the earth" (2:255).

And his saying (peace be upon him): "And nothing that Allah created in His dominion (ملكوته) exits these four," seems to be an allusion to the four colors mentioned at the beginning of his speech (peace be upon him), and a discussion regarding them will follow in the narrations of the Ascension (المعراج), if Allah wills.

And his saying (peace be upon him): "And it is the dominion (الملكوت) which Allah showed to His chosen ones," means that the Throne is the dominion (الملكوت) — *the inner, spiritual realm of sovereignty that governs the outward world* — except that the dominion is two: a higher dominion (ملكوت أعلى) and a lower dominion (ملكوت أسفل). Since the Throne is the station of synthesis (الإجمال) and the inner aspect of the two gates of the Unseen (الغيب), as will be shown by narrations later, it is more appropriate for it to be the higher dominion.

And his saying (peace be upon him): "And how could the carriers of the Throne carry Allah..." etc., is a reinforcement and confirmation of the beginning of the discourse: that the Throne is the station of carrying the existence of things and sustaining them. Thus, the carriers of the Throne are carried by Him, the Glorified, not carriers of Him. How could it be otherwise, when their existence and the course of their existence are sustained by Him, the Exalted, and not by themselves? Because he (peace be upon him) considered this existential station as knowledge (علما), he expressed their existence and the perfection of their existence through "hearts" (القلوب) and the "light of guidance" (نور الاهتداء) to the gnosis of Allah, as he said: "And by His life their hearts lived, and by His light they were guided to His gnosis."

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of transmission to Hannan ibn Sadir, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the Throne (العرش) and the Pedestal (الكرسي), and he said: **إن للعرش صفات كثيرة مختلفة ، له في كل سبب وضع في القرآن صفة على حدة ـ فقوله : « رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ » يقول : رب الملك العظيم ، وقوله : « الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى » يقول : على الملك احتوى ، وهذا علم الكيفوفية في الأشياء.** — "Indeed, the Throne has many different attributes; for every context (سبب) placed in the Quran, it has a distinct attribute. So His saying: 'Lord of the Great Throne' (9:129) means: Lord of the great kingdom (الملك). And His saying: 'The All-Beneficent, settled on the Throne' (20:5) means: He encompassed the kingdom. And this is the knowledge of the 'howness' (علم الكيفوفية) in things."

**ثم العرش في الوصل مفرد عن الكرسي ـ لأنهما بابان من أكبر أبواب الغيوب ـ وهما جميعا غيبان ، وهما في الغيب مقرونان ـ لأن الكرسي هو الباب الظاهر من الغيب ـ الذي منه مطلع البدع ومنها الأشياء كلها ، والعرش هو الباطن الذي يوجد فيه علم الكيف ـ والكون والقدر والحد والأين والمشية وصفة الإرادة ـ وعلم الألفاظ والحركات والترك وعلم العود والبدء.** — "Furthermore, the Throne, when joined [in discourse], is distinct from the Pedestal, because they are two of the greatest gates of the Unseen (الغيوب). Both are unseen, and in the Unseen they are coupled, because the Pedestal is the manifest gate (الباب الظاهر) of the Unseen, from which is the point of emergence of innovations (البدع) and from which all things originate. The Throne is the hidden gate (الباطن) in which is found the knowledge of howness (الكيف), being (الكون), measure (القدر), limit (الحد), where-ness (الأين), volition (المشية), the attribute of will (صفة الإرادة), the knowledge of words (الألفاظ), movements (الحركات), cessation (الترك), and the knowledge of return (العود) and beginning (البدء)."

**فهما في العلم بابان مقرونان ـ لأن ملك العرش سوى ملك الكرسي ، وعلمه أغيب من علم الكرسي فمن ذلك قال : « رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ » أي صفته أعظم من صفة الكرسي ، وهما في ذلك مقرونان.** — "So in knowledge, they are two coupled gates, because the dominion of the Throne is other than the dominion of the Pedestal, and its knowledge is more hidden than the knowledge of the Pedestal. For that reason He said: 'Lord of the Great Throne,' meaning its attribute is greater than the attribute of the Pedestal, and in that, they are coupled."

**قلت : جعلت فداك فلم صار في الفضل جار الكرسي؟ قال عليه‌السلام : إنه صار جاره لأن علم الكيفوفية فيه ـ وفيه الظاهر من أبواب البداء وإنيتها ـ وحد رتقها وفتقها ـ فهذان جاران أحدهما حمل صاحبه في الصرف ، وبمثل صرف العلماء ، وليستدلوا على صدق دعواهما ـ لأنه يختص برحمته من يشاء وهو القوي العزيز.** — "I said: 'May I be your ransom, why then did it become the neighbor of the Pedestal in merit?' He (peace be upon him) said: 'It became its neighbor because the knowledge of howness is in it, and in it is the manifest aspect of the gates of change in the divine decree (البداء) and their identity (إنيتها), and the limit of their joining (رتقها) and rending (فتقها). So these two are neighbors; one of them carries its companion in the turn of speech (الصرف), and like the discourse (صرف) of the scholars, so that they may infer the truth of their claim—for He singles out for His mercy whom He wills, and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Glorious.'"

**I say:** His saying (peace be upon him): "Indeed, the Throne has many attributes..." etc., supports what we mentioned previously: that the "settling on the Throne" (الاستواء على العرش) is to explain the gathering of the reins of universal governance (التدابير العالمية) with Allah. This is also supported by what is at the end of the hadith: "and like the discourse (صرف) of the scholars."

And his saying (peace be upon him): "And this is the knowledge of howness (علم الكيفوفية) in things," refers to the knowledge of the higher causes (العلل العالية) and the ultimate reasons (الأسباب القصوى) for existents. For the word "how" (كيف) in common usage, just as it is used to ask about the accident (العرض) known technically as "quality" (الكيف), is also used to ask about the cause of a thing and its "why" (لمه). It is said: "How did such a thing come to exist?" and "How did Zayd do such a thing when he is unable?"

And his saying (peace be upon him): "Furthermore, the Throne, when joined, is distinct from the Pedestal..." etc., his meaning is that the Throne and the Pedestal are one in the sense that they are the station of the Unseen (مقام الغيب) from which things appear and descend to this world. However, in the linguistic connection (الصلة الكلامية), the Throne is distinguished from the Pedestal because this station in itself is divided into two stations and branches into two gates, yet they are coupled and not disparate. One is the manifest gate (الباب الظاهر) that borders this world, and the other is the hidden gate (الباب الباطن) that follows it. Then he explained it by saying: "Because the Pedestal is the manifest gate..." etc.

His saying (peace be upon him): "Because the Pedestal is the manifest gate from which is the point of emergence of innovations (البدع) and from which all things originate," means the emergence of matters that are innovative (البديعة) without a prior model. From it, all things are realized because all of them are innovative without a prior model. They are only innovative when their realization is not expected from the previous state that produced the matters preceding this event—those which pass away while this event takes their place. Thus, the matter reverts to change in the divine decree (البداء) — *the manifestation of a new divine will that replaces a previously apparent one* — by erasing the ruling of one cause and establishing the ruling of another in its place. All events occurring in this world that are based on the operation of competing causes (الأسباب المتزاحمة) and opposing forces (القوى المتضادة) are innovative occurrences and "changes" (بداءات) in the will.

Above these competing causes and varying wills that constantly struggle for existence, there is a single Cause and a single governing Will; nothing occurs except what He wills. He is the one who veils this cause with that cause, changes the ruling of this will, and restricts the absolute influence of everything by another. This is like one who intends to travel a road for a certain purpose and sets out on it; while he is traveling the road, he sometimes stops to rest for a while. The cause of stopping might conflict with the cause of traveling and movement, causing it to cease operation, and one will changes another will. However, there is another will that governs both wills and organizes the action according to what it inclines toward, by prioritizing this one at one time and that one at another. The two wills—meaning the causes of movement and rest—even if each works for itself and on its own, competing with its companion, they both agree in obedience to the Will that is above them, and they cooperate in executing what is necessitated by the Cause that is higher and loftier than them.

Thus, the station where the two conflicting causes are separated and from which their competition originates is like the Pedestal (الكرسي). The station where it becomes apparent that they are harmonious and reconciled is like the Throne (العرش). It is clear that the second is prior to the first, and that they differ by a kind of synthesis (إجمال) and detail (تفصيل), and hiddenness (بطون) and manifestation (ظهور).

It is most appropriate for these two stations to be called "Throne" (عرش) and "Pedestal" (كرسي) because they contain the properties of a king's throne and his pedestal. For the pedestal is that upon which the king's decrees appear through his agents and working hands, each of whom works independently in some type of the kingdom's affairs. Sometimes the pedestals conflict, so the ruling of some is prioritized over others, and some abrogate the ruling of others. However, they all agree and unite in obedience to the decrees of the Throne, which is exclusive to the king himself. With him is the decree preserved from the conflict of causes and not abrogated by the abrogation of agents and hands. In his Throne is the synthesis of all details and the inner reality of what appears from the side of the agents and hands.

By this explanation, the meaning of his saying (peace be upon him) becomes clear: "Because the Pedestal is the manifest gate..." etc. So his saying "from which is the point of emergence of innovations" means the emergence of existential matters not preceded by a like. And his saying "and from which all things originate" means the details of creation and its various, scattered particulars.

And his saying: "And the Throne is the hidden gate," is in contrast to the Pedestal being the manifest gate. Hiddenness and manifestation in them are in consideration of the occurrence of divergence in the issued decrees or the lack thereof. And his saying "in which is found..." etc., means all the sciences and forms (الصور) that the details of things eventually return to in their synthesis.

And his saying: "knowledge of howness (علم الكيف)," it seems the intended meaning of "howness" is the specificity of a thing's issuance from its causes. And his saying: "and being (الكون)," refers to the entirety of its existence, just as "return and beginning" (العود والبدء) refers to the first of the existences of things and their end. And his saying: "and measure and limit" (القدر والحد), the two refer to one thing, except that "measure" (القدر) is the state of the thing's quantity in itself, while "limit" (الحد) is the state of the thing in its relation to others and its prevention from entering its own domain and mixing with it. And his saying: "and where-ness" (الأين), is the spatial relation. And his saying: "and volition and the attribute of will" (المشية وصفة الإرادة), they are one; it is possible that "volition" refers to its origin and "the attribute of will" to its specificity.

And his saying: "and the knowledge of words, movements, and cessation," the knowledge of words is the knowledge of how the indication (دلالة) of words originates through their connection to the external world according to nature (الطبع), for conventional indication (الدلالة الوضعية) ultimately traces back to nature. The knowledge of movements and cessation is the knowledge of actions and omissions in terms of their connection to essences. It is possible that the entirety of his saying: "knowledge of words and knowledge of movements and cessation" refers to the knowledge of how the mental constructs (اعتبارات) of commands and prohibitions originate from actions and omissions, and how languages originate from their realities that trace back to a single source. "Cessation" (الترك) is relative rest in contrast to movements.

And his saying: "because the knowledge of howness is in it," the pronoun refers to the Throne. And his saying: "and in it is the manifest aspect of the gates of change in the divine decree (البداء)," the pronoun refers to the Pedestal. *Bada’* (البداء) is the manifestation of one cause over another and the nullification of its effect, and it applies to all varying existential causes in terms of their influence.

And his saying (peace be upon him): "So these two are neighbors; one of them carries its companion in the turn of speech (الصرف)," means—as supported by the previous explanation—that the Throne and the Pedestal are compatible neighbors, rather a single reality differing according to the two levels of synthesis and detail. One was only attributed to carrying the other according to the turn of speech and the striking of a metaphor, and through metaphors, subtle and obscure knowledge is clarified for the scholars.

And his saying: "so that they may infer the truth of their claim," meaning the claim of the Throne and the Pedestal; that is, this metaphor was made a means for scholars to infer the truth of the correct knowledge presented to them regarding how the governance (التدبير) running in the world originates from the stations of synthesis and detail, and the hidden and the manifest. So understand that.

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of transmission to al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), that he was asked about the saying of the Exalted: **وَكَانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْمَاءِ** — "and His Throne was upon the waters" (11:7). He said: "What do they say?" It was said: "That the Throne was upon the water and the Lord was above it!" He said: **كذبوا ، من زعم هذا ـ فقد صير الله محمولا ووصفه بصفة المخلوقين ، ولزمه أن الشيء الذي يحمله هو أقوى منه. قال : إن الله حمل دينه وعلمه الماء ـ قبل أن تكون سماء أو أرض ـ أو جن أو إنس أو شمس أو قمر.** — "They have lied. Whoever claims this has made Allah a carried object and described Him with the attribute of created beings, and it would follow that the thing carrying Him is stronger than Him." He said: "Indeed, Allah made the water carry His religion and His knowledge before there was a heaven or earth, or jinn or human, or sun or moon."

**I say:** This is like the previous one in indicating that the Throne is knowledge (العلم), and the water is the origin of creation, and the active knowledge was related to it before the appearance of details.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from Ali (peace be upon him), that he was asked about the distance between the earth and the Throne. He said: **قول العبد مخلصا : لا إله إلا الله.** — "The servant's saying with sincerity: 'There is no god but Allah'."

**I say:** This is among the subtleties of his speech (peace be upon him), taking it from the saying of the Exalted: **إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ** — "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous deeds" (35:10). The reason for this is that when the servant negates divinity from other than Him, the Exalted, by sincerely dedicating divinity and independence to Him, the Exalted, it necessitates forgetting other than Him and turning toward the station of everything's dependence upon Him, the Exalted. This is the station of the Throne according to what has been explained.

Similar to it in subtlety is his saying (peace be upon him) when asked about the distance between the earth and the sky: "The reach of the sight and the prayer of the oppressed."

In *al-Faqih*, *al-Majalis*, and *al-Ilal* by al-Saduq: It is narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he was asked: "Why was the Kaaba named Kaaba?" He said: "Because it is square (مربعة)." It was said to him: "And why did it become square?" He said: "Because it is aligned with the Inhabited House (البيت المعمور), which is square." It was said to him: "And why did the Inhabited House become square?" He said: "Because it is aligned with the Throne, which is square." It was said to him: "And why did the Throne become square?" He said: **لأن الكلمات التي بني عليها الإسلام أربع : سبحان الله ، والحمد لله ، ولا إله إلا الله ، والله أكبر.** — "Because the words upon which Islam is built are four: Glory be to Allah (سبحان الله), Praise be to Allah (والحمد لله), There is no god but Allah (لا إله إلا الله), and Allah is Greater (والله أكبر)." [The hadith continues].
