**I say:** These four words—the first of them includes transcendence (التنزيه) and sanctification (التقديس); the second, immanence (التشبيه) and praise (الثناء); the third, monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — which combines transcendence and immanence; and the fourth, the greatest monotheism specific to Islam, which is that Allah, the Glorified, is greater than can be described. For description is a restriction and a limitation, and He, the Exalted, is too majestic for any limit to bound Him or any restriction to constrain Him. A portion of the discussion on this has already preceded in the commentary on the saying of the Exalted: **لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَاثَةٍ** — "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity’" (5:73).

In short, the meaning reverts to its interpretation as knowledge (العلم) as previously mentioned. The various narrations in this sense are many, such as what has been reported that the Verse of the Pedestal (آية الكرسي), the end of *al-Baqarah*, and Surah Muhammad are from the treasures of the Throne; and what has been reported that صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم is a river that comes out from the leg of the Throne; and what has been reported that the "Manifest Horizon" (الأفق المبين) is a plain before the Throne in which there are rivers flowing with vessels numbering the stars.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, from Abd al-Rahim al-Aqsar, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: I asked him about **ن وَالْقَلَمِ** (Nun. By the Pen). He said: **إن الله خلق القلم من شجرة في الجنة ـ يقال لها : الخلد ، ثم قال لنهر في الجنة : كن مدادا فجمد النهر ، وكان أشد بياضا من الثلج وأحلى من الشهد. ثم قال للقلم : اكتب. قال : يا رب ما أكتب؟ قال : اكتب ما كان وما هو كائن إلى يوم القيامة فكتب القلم في رق أشد بياضا من الفضة ـ وأصفى من الياقوت ـ ثم طواه فجعله في ركن العرش ـ ثم ختم على فم القلم فلم ينطق بعد ، ولا ينطق أبدا ـ فهو الكتاب المكنون الذي منه النسخ كلها.** — "Indeed, Allah created the Pen from a tree in Paradise called 'Eternity' (الخلد). Then He said to a river in Paradise: 'Be ink,' and the river solidified, and it was whiter than snow and sweeter than honey. Then He said to the Pen: 'Write.' It said: 'O Lord, what shall I write?' He said: 'Write what was and what is to be until the Day of Resurrection.' So the Pen wrote on a parchment whiter than silver and clearer than ruby. Then He folded it and placed it in a corner of the Throne. Then He sealed the mouth of the Pen, so it did not speak thereafter, and it shall never speak. It is the Hidden Book (الكتاب المكنون) from which all copies (النسخ) are made." [The hadith continues]. Its completion will come in Surah *Nun*, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning. In some of them, when the narrator sought more explanation and persisted, he (peace be upon him) said: "The Pen is an angel and the Tablet (اللوح) is an angel." He thereby clarified that what he described was a representation (تمثيل) of the sort of likening the intelligible to the sensible to facilitate understanding of the objective.

In the book *Rawdat al-Wa'izin*, from al-Sadiq, from his father, from his grandfather (peace be upon them), who said: **في العرش تمثال ما خلق الله في البر والبحر.** — "In the Throne is an image (تمثال) of whatever Allah created in the land and the sea." He said: "And this is the interpretation of His saying: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ** — 'There is not a thing but that its primary sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure' (15:21)."

**I say:** That is, the existence of the forms (صور) of things and their images in the Throne is the reality upon which the explanation of the verse is built. The clarification of the meaning of the existence of the forms of things in the Throne has already preceded. In the sense of this narration is what has been reported in the commentary on the supplication "O He who manifest the beautiful."

In it also, from al-Sadiq, from his father, from his grandfather (peace be upon them), in a hadith: **وإن بين القائمة من قوائم العرش والقائمة الثانية ـ خفقان الطير المسرع مسير ألف عام ، والعرش يكسى كل يوم سبعين ألف لون من النور ـ لا يستطيع أن ينظر إليه خلق من خلق الله ، والأشياء كلها في العرش كحلقة في فلاة.** — "And indeed, between one pillar of the pillars of the Throne and the second pillar is the flight of a swift bird for a journey of a thousand years. The Throne is clothed every day with seventy thousand colors of light; no creature among the creatures of Allah can look at it. And all things in the Throne are like a ring in a vast desert."

**I say:** The last sentence is among what has been narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم through the paths of both the Shiah and the Sunnis. What he (peace be upon him) mentioned, based on what has preceded, is a representation (تمثيل), and its likes are many in their narrations (peace be upon them).

Evidence for this is that the greatness of the Throne described in the narration, by whatever calculation is assumed, is far exceeded by the circles drawn by light rays. Thus, the description is only to bring the intelligible closer to the sensible.

In *al-Ilal*, from the *Ilal* of Muhammad ibn Sinan, from al-Rida (peace be upon him): **علة الطواف بالبيت أن الله تبارك وتعالى ـ قال للملائكة « إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً ـ قالُوا أَتَجْعَلُ فِيها مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فِيها وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّماءَ » ، فردوا على الله تبارك وتعالى هذا الجواب ـ فعلموا أنهم أذنبوا فندموا فلاذوا بالعرش واستغفروا ـ فأحب الله عز وجل أن يتعبد بمثل ذلك العباد ـ فوضع في السماء الرابعة بيتا بحذاء العرش ـ يسمى « الضراح » ثم وضع في السماء الدنيا بيتا ـ يسمى « البيت المعمور » بحذاء الضراح ـ ثم وضع البيت بحذاء البيت المعمور ـ ثم أمر آدم فطاف به ـ فجرى في ولده إلى يوم القيامة.** — "The reason for circumambulating the House is that Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, said to the angels: 'Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth.' They said: 'Will You set in it such as will cause corruption in it and shed blood?' (2:30). They returned this answer to Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, then they realized they had sinned, so they felt regret, sought refuge in the Throne, and asked for forgiveness. So Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, loved that the servants should worship Him with the like of that. Thus, He placed a house in the fourth heaven aligned with the Throne called 'al-Durah' (الضراح). Then He placed a house in the lower heaven called 'the Inhabited House' (البيت المعمور) aligned with al-Durah. Then He placed the House [the Kaaba] aligned with the Inhabited House. Then He commanded Adam to circumambulate it, and so it continued among his children until the Day of Resurrection." [The hadith continues].

**I say:** The hadith is not devoid of strangeness from several aspects. In any case, based on the interpretation of the Throne as knowledge (العلم), the meaning of the angels seeking refuge in the Throne is their admission of ignorance and returning knowledge to Him, the Glorified, where they said: **سُبْحَانَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَا إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "Immune are You from any flaw! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise" (2:32). The discussion on this story has already passed at the beginning of Surah *al-Baqarah*. In the narration, *al-Durah* and the Inhabited House are mentioned in the heavens, while most narrations mention only one house in the heavens, which is the Inhabited House in the fourth heaven. In it is also the attribution of sin to the angels, while they are infallible (معصومون) by the explicit text of the Quran; perhaps what is meant by the knowledge of sin is the knowledge of a kind of deficiency.

As for the Kaaba being aligned with the Inhabited House, it is apparent that this is a spiritual alignment (محاذاة معنوية), not a sensible, physical one. Evidence for this is his saying: "So He placed a house in the fourth heaven aligned with the Throne," since what is gathered from the Quran and hadith is that the Throne and the Pedestal encompass the heavens and the earth, and the meaning of alignment between the encompasser (المحيط) and the encompassed (المحاط) is not realized if the encompassment is physical.

In *al-Khisal*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): **أن حملة العرش أحدهم على صورة ابن آدم ـ يسترزق الله لولد آدم. والثاني على صورة الديك يسترزق الله للطير ، والثالث على صورة الأسد يسترزق الله للسباع ، والرابع على صورة الثور يسترزق الله للبهائم ، ونكس الثور رأسه منذ عبد بنو إسرائيل العجل ـ فإذا كان يوم القيامة صاروا ثمانية.** — "Indeed, of the carriers of the Throne, one is in the form of a human—he seeks provision from Allah for the children of Adam. The second is in the form of a rooster—he seeks provision from Allah for the birds. The third is in the form of a lion—he seeks provision from Allah for the beasts of prey. The fourth is in the form of a bull—he seeks provision from Allah for the cattle. The bull has lowered its head ever since the Children of Israel worshipped the calf. When the Day of Resurrection comes, they will become eight." [The report continues].

**I say:** The reports with a meaning close to this are many and widely transmitted. In some of them, these four are counted as carriers of the Pedestal (الكرسي), and it is the only report that mentions carriers for the Pedestal—among what we have found—and we have cited it in the commentary on the Verse of the Pedestal in Surah *al-Baqarah*.

In another hadith: **حملة العرش ثمانية : أربعة من الأولين وأربعة من الآخرين : فأما الأربعة من الأولين ـ فنوح وإبراهيم وموسى وعيسى ، وأما الأربعة من الآخرين : فمحمد وعلي والحسن والحسين عليه‌السلام.** — "The carriers of the Throne are eight: four from the former ones and four from the latter ones. As for the four from the former ones, they are Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus. As for the four from the latter ones, they are Muhammad, Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn (peace be upon them)."

**I say:** Based on the interpretation of the Throne as knowledge (العلم), there is no harm in four of the angels being counted as its carriers, and then a number of others being counted as its carriers.

The narrations regarding the Throne are many and scattered throughout various chapters, and they support what has passed regarding its interpretation as knowledge. Those among them that have some appearance of physicality are interpreted by what has preceded. As for the Throne being a physical body in the form of a bed placed upon the seventh heaven, this is something that no hadith worthy of consideration indicates; rather, there are narrations that contradict it, such as the first narration mentioned above.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ** — "created the heavens and the earth in six days" (7:54). He said: He (peace be upon him) said: "In six periods (أوقات)."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*: The author of *Thaqib al-Manaqib* narrated it with his chain to Abu Hashim al-Ja'fari from Muhammad ibn Salih al-Armani, who said: I said to Abu Muhammad al-Askari (peace be upon him): "Inform me about the saying of Allah: **لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ مِن قَبْلُ وَمِن بَعْدُ** — 'All command belongs to Allah, before as well as after' (30:4)." He said: **لله الأمر من قبل أن يأمر ـ ومن بعد أن يأمر ما يشاء.** — "To Allah belongs the command before He commands and after He commands whatever He wills." I said to myself: "This is the interpretation of the saying of Allah: **أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ تَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ** — 'Look! All creation and command belong to Him. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds' (7:54)." He turned to me and said: "It is as you kept secret within yourself: 'Look! All creation and command belong to Him. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds'."

**I say:** Its meaning is that His saying: "Look! All creation and command belong to Him" signifies the absolute nature of ownership (الملك) before the issuance [of the command] and after it, unlike us, where we own the command—in what we own—before its issuance, but once it is issued, it leaves our ownership and choice.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir extracted from Abd al-Aziz al-Shami from his father, who was a companion, that he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: **من لم يحمد الله على ما عمل من عمل صالح وحمد نفسه ـ فقد كفر وحبط ما عمل ، ومن زعم أن الله جعل للعباد من الأمر شيئا ـ فقد كفر بما أنزل الله على أنبيائه لقوله : « أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ تَبارَكَ اللهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ ».** — "Whoever does not praise Allah for a righteous deed he has performed and instead praises himself, has indeed committed ingratitude (كفر) and his deed is nullified. And whoever claims that Allah has granted the servants any part of the command [independently], has indeed disbelieved in what Allah revealed to His prophets, because of His saying: 'Look! All creation and command belong to Him. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds'."

**I say:** What is meant by "disbelief" (الكفر) through vanity (العجب) is ingratitude for the blessing or for the fact that good deeds belong to Allah, as indicated by the Quran. What is meant by negating that any part of the command belongs to the servants is the negation of it being granted in the manner of independence (الاستقلال), rather than a subordinate (تبعي) form of ownership and command.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission to Muyassir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: I said [regarding] the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He: **وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَاحِهَا** — "And do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration" (7:56). He said: **يا ميسر إن الأرض كانت فاسدة ـ فأحياها الله عز وجل بنبيه ، ولا تفسدوا في الأرض بعد إصلاحها.** — "O Muyassir, indeed the earth was corrupt, so Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, gave it life through His Prophet; so do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration."

**I say:** Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Muyassir from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) as a disconnected report.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Nasa'i extracted from Abu Musa, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: **مثل ما بعثني الله به من الهدى والعلم ـ كمثل الغيث الكثير أصاب أرضا ـ فكانت منها بقية فبلت الماء ـ فأنبتت الكلأ والعشب الكثير ، وكانت منها أجادب أمسكت الماء ـ فنفع الله بها الناس فشربوا وسقوا وزرعوا ، وأصاب منها طائفة أخرى ـ إنما هي قيعان لا تمسك ماء ولا تنبت كلأ ـ فذلك مثل من فقه في دين الله ـ ونفعه ما بعثني الله به فعلم وعلم ، ومثل من لم يرفع بذلك رأسا ـ ولم يقبل هدى الله الذي أرسلت به.** — "The likeness of the guidance (الهدى) and knowledge (العلم) with which Allah has sent me is like a heavy rain that falls upon a land. Some of it was fertile soil that absorbed the water and brought forth much fodder and grass. Some of it was hard ground that held the water, and Allah benefited the people by it, so they drank, gave drink to others, and sowed crops. And some of it fell upon another part, which was only barren plains that neither hold water nor bring forth fodder. That is the likeness of one who gains understanding (فقه) in the religion of Allah and is benefited by what Allah has sent me with, so he learns and teaches; and the likeness of one who does not raise his head for it and does not accept the guidance of Allah with which I have been sent."
